name: Rava Upma
steps: Place a heavy-bottomed pan on medium heat. Add the rava and dry roast it for about 4-5 minutes, stirring continuously until it turns light golden and releases a nutty aroma. Transfer to a plate and set aside.
In the same pan, heat 2 tablespoons of oil over medium heat. Once hot, add the mustard seeds and wait for them to splutter — this should take about 15-20 seconds.
Add the curry leaves and slit green chilies to the oil. Sauté for about 10 seconds until the curry leaves turn crisp and aromatic.
Add the finely chopped onion and sauté for 3-4 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the onion turns soft and translucent.
Pour in 2.5 cups of water and add salt. Bring the water to a rolling boil over high heat.
Once the water is boiling vigorously, reduce the flame to low. Slowly add the roasted rava in a thin stream with one hand while stirring continuously with the other to prevent any lumps from forming.
Once all the rava is incorporated, cover the pan with a lid and cook on the lowest flame for 3-4 minutes until all the water is absorbed and the upma has a fluffy texture.
Turn off the heat, squeeze half a lemon over the upma, and fluff it gently with a fork. Serve hot.
tips: Roasting the rava well is the key to non-sticky upma. Always add rava to boiling water, not the other way around. If you see lumps, press them against the side of the pan with your spoon.

name: Kanda Poha
steps: Place the thick poha in a colander and rinse under running water for just 5-10 seconds. Shake off excess water immediately — do not soak. The poha should be moist but not mushy.
Add turmeric, salt, and sugar to the drained poha. Toss gently with your fingers to coat evenly without breaking the flakes. Set aside.
Heat 2 tablespoons of oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Add the mustard seeds and wait until they start to pop and splutter.
Add the raw peanuts to the oil and fry them for about 1-2 minutes, stirring frequently, until they turn light golden brown and crunchy.
Toss in the curry leaves and slit green chilies. Let them sizzle for 10 seconds until fragrant.
Add the finely chopped onions and sauté for 2-3 minutes until they soften but still have a slight bite — you don't want them completely mushy.
Lower the flame and gently add the seasoned poha into the pan. Use a flat spatula to fold it in carefully so the flakes don't break. Mix everything together evenly.
Cover the pan and let it cook on low heat for 2 minutes so the poha heats through and absorbs all the flavors.
Remove from heat, squeeze fresh lemon juice over the top, and garnish generously with chopped coriander leaves. Serve immediately while still warm.
tips: The secret to perfect poha is never soaking it — just a quick rinse. The residual moisture is exactly enough to soften the flakes during cooking. Adding sugar is traditional Maharashtrian style and balances the flavors beautifully.

name: Pancakes
steps: In a large mixing bowl, whisk together the flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt until evenly combined.
In a separate bowl, whisk the milk, melted butter (cooled slightly), and egg until smooth.

Pour the wet ingredients into the dry ingredients and stir gently with a wooden spoon or spatula. Mix just until the ingredients come together — there should still be small lumps in the batter. Overmixing develops gluten and makes pancakes tough.
Let the batter rest for 5 minutes. This allows the baking powder to activate and the flour to hydrate, resulting in fluffier pancakes.
Heat a non-stick pan or griddle over medium heat. Add a small pat of butter and let it melt, tilting the pan to coat evenly.
Pour approximately 1/4 cup of batter for each pancake. Cook until you see bubbles forming on the surface and the edges start to look set — about 2-3 minutes.
Flip the pancake with a spatula and cook the other side for another 1-2 minutes until golden brown.
Repeat with remaining batter, adding a little butter to the pan between batches. Stack the pancakes and serve with maple syrup, fresh berries, or a pat of butter on top.
tips: The number one mistake people make is overmixing the batter — lumps are your friend! Also, if the first pancake doesn't turn out perfect, that's completely normal. The pan needs to reach the right temperature, and the first one is always the test pancake.

name: French Toast
steps: Crack the eggs into a wide, shallow bowl or dish — something wide enough to dip a whole bread slice in.
Add the milk, cinnamon, and sugar to the eggs. Whisk everything together vigorously until the mixture is completely smooth and the sugar is dissolved.
Heat a non-stick pan or griddle over medium heat. Add about 1 tablespoon of butter and swirl the pan to coat the surface evenly as it melts.

Take one bread slice and place it in the egg mixture. Let it soak for about 10-15 seconds, then carefully flip it and soak the other side for another 10-15 seconds. The bread should be well-coated but not falling apart.
Lift the soaked bread, letting any excess mixture drip off for a moment, then place it gently in the hot buttered pan.
Cook undisturbed for 2-3 minutes until the bottom turns a beautiful golden brown. Check by lifting a corner with your spatula.
Flip carefully and cook the other side for another 2 minutes until equally golden. The toast should feel slightly firm but still custardy inside.
Repeat with remaining slices, adding more butter to the pan as needed. Serve immediately topped with maple syrup, powdered sugar, fresh fruit, or a dollop of whipped cream.
tips: Day-old bread that's slightly stale actually works better than fresh bread — it absorbs the egg mixture without falling apart. If you only have fresh bread, lightly toast the slices first. Brioche or challah bread makes the most indulgent French toast.

name: Masala Dosa
steps: Wash and soak the rice and urad dal separately in plenty of water for at least 6 hours or overnight.
Grind the urad dal first with minimal water to a smooth, fluffy batter — it should be light and airy when you drop some in water, it should float. Then grind the rice to a slightly coarse paste.

Mix both batters together in a large container, add salt, and mix well with your hand. Cover loosely and keep in a warm place to ferment for 8-12 hours until the batter doubles in volume and smells slightly sour.
For the potato filling: boil 4 potatoes until tender, peel and mash them coarsely — you want some chunks for texture, not a smooth puree.
Heat oil in a pan, add mustard seeds. When they pop, add curry leaves, slit green chilies, and chopped onions. Sauté until the onions turn soft and translucent, about 3-4 minutes.
Add turmeric and salt to the onion mixture. Then add the mashed potatoes and mix everything together well. Add a splash of water if it seems dry. Cook for 2-3 minutes so the flavors meld. Set aside.
Heat a cast iron or non-stick tava on medium-high heat. Test if it's ready by sprinkling a few drops of water — they should sizzle and evaporate immediately. Wipe the tava clean with an oiled cloth.
Pour a ladleful of batter in the center of the tava and quickly spread it outward in a circular motion using the back of the ladle, making it as thin as possible.
Drizzle oil or ghee around the edges and on top of the dosa. Cook on medium-high heat until the bottom turns golden and crispy and the edges start lifting off the tava.
Place a portion of the potato filling in the center, fold the dosa over it (either in half or into a roll), and serve immediately with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: Good fermentation is the single most important factor for great dosas. The batter should smell slightly tangy and have visible air bubbles. If your kitchen is cold, place the batter inside your oven with just the light on, or near a warm spot. The tava temperature is critical — too hot and the batter won't spread, too cool and it sticks.

name: Aloo Paratha
steps: Add flour and a pinch of salt to a large bowl. Gradually add water while kneading to form a soft, pliable dough. It should not be sticky. Cover with a damp cloth and let it rest for at least 15 minutes.
While the dough rests, boil the potatoes until completely tender when pierced with a knife. Drain, let cool slightly, then peel and mash thoroughly — no lumps should remain.
To the mashed potatoes, add finely chopped green chilies, chopped coriander leaves, cumin seeds, red chili powder, and salt. Mix everything together well with your hands until evenly combined.
Divide the dough into equal-sized balls (about golf ball size) and the potato filling into the same number of slightly smaller portions.
Take a dough ball, flatten it slightly, and roll it into a small circle about 3-4 inches in diameter. Place a portion of filling in the center.
Gather the edges of the dough up and around the filling, pinching them together at the top to seal completely. Flatten the sealed ball gently with your palm, dust with dry flour.
Roll out carefully into a flat circle about 7-8 inches in diameter, rotating the paratha as you roll and dusting with flour as needed to prevent sticking. Be gentle to avoid the filling bursting out.
Heat a tava or flat griddle on medium-high heat. Place the rolled paratha on the hot tava. Cook for about 1 minute until you see small bubbles forming on the surface.
Flip the paratha. Apply ghee generously on the cooked side. Flip again and apply ghee on the other side too. Press gently with a spatula, especially on the edges, to ensure even cooking.
Cook until both sides have golden brown spots. The paratha should feel slightly crispy on the outside but still soft inside. Serve hot with fresh curd, pickle, and a pat of butter.
tips: The filling should be completely dry — any moisture will make the paratha tear while rolling. Don't over-stuff — start with less filling and increase as you get comfortable. If the dough tears while rolling, patch it with a small piece of dough and continue.

name: Scrambled Eggs
steps: Crack all 4 eggs into a bowl. Add the milk, salt, and a pinch of black pepper. Whisk vigorously with a fork until the yolks and whites are completely blended and slightly frothy.
Place a non-stick pan on low-to-medium heat — this is important, scrambled eggs should never be cooked on high heat. Add the butter and let it melt slowly, tilting the pan to coat the surface.
Once the butter is melted and starts to foam (but before it browns), pour in the egg mixture.
Let the eggs sit undisturbed for about 30 seconds. You'll see the edges start to set and turn opaque while the center is still liquid.
Using a silicone spatula, gently push the cooked edges toward the center, allowing the uncooked egg to flow to the empty space on the pan. Do this slowly and gently — don't stir frantically.
Continue this gentle folding motion every 15-20 seconds. The eggs will form soft, large curds. The whole process should take 3-4 minutes.
Remove the pan from heat when the eggs still look slightly wet and glossy — they will continue cooking from the residual heat of the pan and will be perfectly done by the time you plate them.
Transfer immediately to a plate. Season with a final crack of black pepper and serve right away — scrambled eggs don't wait for anyone.
tips: The biggest mistake is cooking eggs on too-high heat, which makes them rubbery. Low and slow is the golden rule. Remove them from the pan while they're still slightly underdone — they'll finish cooking on the plate. Gordon Ramsay takes his on and off the heat repeatedly for the creamiest results.

name: Avocado Toast
steps: Toast the bread slices in a toaster or under the broiler until they're golden and crispy on the outside but still slightly soft inside.
While the bread toasts, cut the avocado in half lengthwise. Twist the two halves to separate them and remove the pit by carefully tapping it with the heel of your knife and twisting it out.
Scoop the avocado flesh into a bowl using a large spoon. Add a squeeze of fresh lemon juice (this adds flavor and prevents browning), salt, and black pepper.
Mash the avocado with a fork to your preferred consistency — some people like it smooth, others prefer it chunky with visible pieces.
Divide the mashed avocado between the two toast slices and spread it evenly, making sure to reach the edges.
Sprinkle red chili flakes on top for a kick of heat. Optionally, top with a fried or poached egg, cherry tomato halves, or a drizzle of olive oil.
Serve immediately — avocado toast is best enjoyed fresh.
tips: Choose an avocado that yields to gentle pressure but isn't mushy — that's the perfect ripeness. To speed up ripening, put an unripe avocado in a paper bag with a banana for 1-2 days. The lemon juice isn't just for flavor — it prevents the avocado from turning brown.

name: Egg Bhurji
steps: Finely chop the onion, tomato, and green chilies. Keep them ready on a plate as the cooking happens fast.
Heat oil in a non-stick pan over medium heat. Add the chopped onions and green chilies. Sauté for 2-3 minutes until the onions turn soft and slightly golden.
Add the chopped tomatoes, turmeric, and salt. Cook for 2-3 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes soften and start breaking down into a rough paste.
Crack all 4 eggs directly into the pan over the masala. Using your spatula, immediately start breaking the yolks and scrambling the eggs into the masala base.
Keep stirring and folding the eggs with the masala on medium heat. The eggs will set quickly — this should take about 2 minutes.
Once the eggs are mostly set but still look slightly moist on top, turn off the heat. The residual heat will finish cooking them perfectly.
Garnish generously with fresh chopped coriander leaves. Serve immediately with hot buttered pav (bread rolls), chapati, or just as is.
tips: The key difference between bhurji and regular scrambled eggs is the masala base — you cook the spices first and then add the eggs. Don't overcook the eggs; take them off heat while still slightly wet for the best texture. Some street vendors add a pinch of garam masala at the end for extra punch.

name: Banana Smoothie
steps: Peel the bananas and break them into rough chunks — smaller pieces blend more easily and result in a smoother consistency.
Add the banana chunks, cold milk, yogurt, and honey to a blender jar.
Blend on high speed for about 30-45 seconds until the smoothie is completely smooth and creamy with no visible chunks remaining.
Taste and adjust sweetness — add more honey if needed, or a splash more milk if the consistency is too thick.
Pour into a tall glass and serve immediately. You can top with a few banana slices, a sprinkle of cinnamon, or some granola for extra texture.
tips: For the thickest, creamiest smoothie, freeze your bananas beforehand — peel them, break into chunks, and freeze in a ziplock bag. Frozen bananas give you an almost ice-cream-like texture without any ice. Overripe bananas with brown spots are perfect because they're sweeter.

name: Omelette
steps: Crack the eggs into a bowl, add salt and pepper, and whisk vigorously with a fork until the yolks and whites are completely blended. The more you whisk, the fluffier the omelette.
Finely dice the onion and capsicum into small, even pieces so they cook quickly and distribute evenly in the omelette.
Heat a non-stick pan over medium heat. Add the butter and tilt the pan to coat the entire surface as it melts and starts to foam.
Pour the whisked eggs into the pan. Let the eggs sit undisturbed for about 30 seconds until the bottom sets and turns opaque, while the top is still liquid.
Gently tilt the pan and use a spatula to lift the cooked edges, letting the uncooked egg flow underneath. Do this around the entire edge of the omelette.
When the top surface is still slightly wet but mostly set, sprinkle the diced onion, capsicum, and grated cheese over one half of the omelette.
Using the spatula, carefully fold the other half of the omelette over the fillings. Press down gently and cook for another 30-45 seconds.
Slide the omelette onto a plate — it should be golden on the outside but still soft and slightly creamy inside. Serve immediately.
tips: The pan temperature is crucial — too hot and the eggs brown before the inside is set, too cool and you end up with a rubbery flat egg. Medium heat is the sweet spot. Don't add the fillings until the base has set, or they'll sink to the bottom and make flipping difficult.

name: Medu Vada
steps: Wash the urad dal thoroughly and soak it in plenty of water for 4-5 hours. The dal should be soft enough to break easily between your fingers.

Drain the soaked dal completely. Grind it in a mixer or wet grinder with as little water as possible — add just a tablespoon at a time. The batter should be thick, smooth, and very fluffy. Test by dropping a small bit into a bowl of water — if it floats, the batter is perfectly aerated.
Add finely chopped onion, green chilies, grated ginger, torn curry leaves, crushed black pepper, and salt to the batter. Mix gently without deflating the airiness of the batter.
Heat oil in a deep pan or kadai to about 170°C (340°F). To test, drop a tiny bit of batter in — it should rise to the surface within a couple of seconds.
Wet your hands and take a portion of batter. Shape it into a ball, then make a hole in the center with your wet thumb to form a donut shape. The hole helps the vada cook evenly.
Carefully slide the shaped vada into the hot oil. Fry 2-3 at a time — don't overcrowd the pan as it drops the oil temperature.
Fry on medium heat for about 2-3 minutes per side, flipping once, until the vadas are deep golden brown and crispy all over.
Remove with a slotted spoon and drain on paper towels. Serve hot with coconut chutney and sambar.
tips: The batter consistency is everything — it must be thick enough to hold its shape but fluffy with air. If your batter is too runny, the vadas will absorb oil and be flat. Grinding with minimal water is the secret. Also, keep the oil at a consistent medium temperature; too hot and they burn outside while staying raw inside.

name: Idli
steps: Wash the rice and urad dal separately and soak each in plenty of water for at least 6 hours or overnight.

Grind the urad dal first in a wet grinder or mixer, adding water little by little, until you get a smooth, fluffy, cloud-like batter. This can take 15-20 minutes of grinding. Transfer to a large container.
Next, grind the soaked rice to a slightly coarser paste — it should feel a little grainy between your fingers, not completely smooth like the dal batter.
Combine both batters in a large container (it needs room to expand). Add salt and mix thoroughly with your hand — the warmth of your hand actually helps fermentation.

Cover the container loosely with a lid or plate (don't seal it airtight — the batter needs to breathe) and leave it in a warm spot for 8-12 hours until the batter has doubled in volume and has visible air bubbles on the surface.
When ready to cook, gently stir the fermented batter — don't deflate it too much. The consistency should be thick but pourable, like pancake batter.
Grease the idli molds with a few drops of oil or ghee. Pour batter into each mold until about 3/4 full — the idlis will expand as they steam.
Place the molds in a steamer or idli cooker with enough water at the bottom. Steam on high heat for 10-12 minutes. You'll know they're done when a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean.
Let them cool for a minute, then scoop them out gently using a wet spoon. Serve warm with coconut chutney and piping hot sambar.
tips: Fermentation is the heart of idli-making. If your house is cold, place the batter inside the oven with just the light on, or wrap the container in a warm towel. The batter should smell pleasantly sour when it's ready. If your idlis come out flat, the batter either wasn't fermented enough or was over-stirred after fermentation.

name: Spaghetti Aglio e Olio
steps: Bring a large pot of generously salted water to a rolling boil. Add the spaghetti and cook according to package directions until al dente — it should still have a slight firmness when you bite into it. Before draining, reserve about 1 cup of the starchy pasta water.
While the pasta cooks, peel the garlic cloves and slice them very thinly — uniformly thin slices ensure even cooking so some don't burn while others are undercooked.

Heat the olive oil in a large pan over low-to-medium heat. Add the sliced garlic and chili flakes. Cook very gently, stirring occasionally, until the garlic turns a light golden color — this should take 3-4 minutes. Watch it carefully, as garlic goes from golden to burnt in seconds.
The moment the garlic is golden, remove the pan from the heat to stop the cooking. The residual heat in the oil will continue to infuse the garlic flavor.

Add the drained spaghetti directly into the pan with the garlic oil. Return to low heat and toss everything together, adding splashes of the reserved pasta water as needed — the starch in the water creates a silky sauce that coats each strand.
Toss in the fresh chopped parsley, adjust salt, and give everything a final toss. The pasta should look glossy and every strand should be coated in the fragrant garlic oil.
Serve immediately in warm bowls. This dish waits for nobody.
tips: This is a dish of extreme simplicity, which means every element matters. Use the best olive oil you can afford — you'll taste it. Slice the garlic thin and even, and never let it go past golden. The pasta water is the secret ingredient — it emulsifies with the oil to create a proper sauce rather than just oily noodles.

name: Margherita Pizza
steps: In a small bowl, combine warm water (about 40°C — warm to the touch but not hot), yeast, and sugar. Stir gently and let it sit for about 10 minutes until the mixture becomes foamy and bubbly, which means the yeast is active.
In a large bowl, combine the flour and salt. Make a well in the center and pour in the yeast mixture along with a tablespoon of olive oil.

Mix with a wooden spoon until a shaggy dough forms, then turn it out onto a lightly floured surface. Knead for 8-10 minutes until the dough is smooth, elastic, and springs back when poked with your finger.
Shape the dough into a ball, place it in a lightly oiled bowl, cover with plastic wrap or a damp towel, and let it rise in a warm place for 1 to 1.5 hours until it doubles in size.

While the dough rises, make the sauce: roughly chop the tomatoes and blend them with a pinch of salt, a clove of garlic, and a drizzle of olive oil into a smooth puree. No need to cook it — it will cook in the oven.
Preheat your oven to the highest temperature it will go — ideally 250°C or above. If you have a pizza stone, place it in the oven to preheat as well.

Once the dough has doubled, punch it down to release the air. On a floured surface, stretch it out into a thin circle using your hands — push from the center outward while rotating. Don't use a rolling pin as it pushes out the air bubbles that create those beautiful charred spots.
Transfer the stretched dough to a floured baking sheet or pizza peel. Spread a thin layer of the tomato sauce, leaving about an inch border for the crust.
Tear the mozzarella into chunks and distribute evenly over the sauce. Don't overload — less is more with a Margherita.

Bake for 10-12 minutes until the crust is golden and charred in spots, and the cheese is melted and bubbly with some golden patches. Remove from oven, immediately top with fresh basil leaves and a drizzle of good olive oil. Slice and serve.
tips: The hotter your oven, the better the pizza. A pizza stone makes a huge difference — it absorbs moisture from the dough, giving you a crispier base. True Neapolitan pizza should be slightly charred and blistered. Fresh mozzarella (the kind in water) is far superior to the pre-shredded kind.

name: Penne Arrabbiata
steps: Bring a large pot of well-salted water to a boil. Add the penne and cook until al dente according to package instructions. Reserve a cup of pasta water before draining.

While the pasta cooks, roughly chop the tomatoes. If you want a smoother sauce, score an X on the bottom of each tomato, blanch in boiling water for 30 seconds, then peel off the skins before chopping.
Heat olive oil in a large sauté pan over medium heat. Add the minced garlic and chili flakes. Sauté for about 30 seconds until the garlic is fragrant — don't let it brown.

Add the chopped tomatoes to the pan along with salt. Stir, then let the sauce simmer on medium-low heat for about 10-12 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes break down into a chunky, thick sauce.
Use the back of your spoon to crush any remaining large tomato pieces. The sauce should be thick enough to coat a spoon.
Add the drained penne to the sauce. Toss well, adding splashes of reserved pasta water to loosen the sauce and help it cling to the pasta.
Tear the fresh basil leaves and fold them into the pasta. Give everything one final toss.
Plate the pasta and finish with freshly grated parmesan cheese on top. Serve immediately.
tips: Arrabbiata means 'angry' in Italian — the dish should have a noticeable kick of heat. Adjust the chili to your preference. Using San Marzano tomatoes instead of regular ones will give you a naturally sweeter, richer sauce. The pasta water is essential — it thickens the sauce and helps it adhere to the penne.

name: Rajma
steps: Wash the rajma (red kidney beans) thoroughly and soak them in plenty of water overnight or for at least 8 hours. They should swell to roughly double their original size.

Drain the soaking water and add the rajma to a pressure cooker with fresh water (about 3 cups), a pinch of salt, and a pinch of turmeric. Pressure cook on high for 5-6 whistles, then reduce to low and cook for another 15 minutes until the beans are completely soft and can be easily mashed between two fingers.
While the rajma cooks, prepare the masala. Heat oil in a heavy-bottomed pan over medium heat. Add the cumin seeds and let them splutter and turn aromatic.
Add the finely chopped onions and sauté for 5-6 minutes, stirring frequently, until they turn deep golden brown. Don't rush this step — properly browned onions are the foundation of the flavor.
Add the ginger-garlic paste (or finely minced ginger and garlic) and cook for another minute until the raw smell disappears.

Add the chopped tomatoes along with turmeric, red chili powder, and coriander powder. Cook on medium heat for 6-8 minutes, stirring occasionally, until the tomatoes completely break down and the oil begins to separate from the masala — this is the sign that your masala is properly cooked.
Add the cooked rajma along with all its cooking liquid into the masala. Mix well and bring to a boil.

Reduce the heat to low and let the rajma simmer uncovered for 15-20 minutes, stirring occasionally. The gravy will thicken as it simmers. For a creamier texture, use the back of your ladle to mash some of the beans against the side of the pan.
Add garam masala, adjust salt, and garnish with fresh coriander leaves. Serve hot over steamed basmati rice.
tips: Never cook rajma without soaking — unsoaked or undercooked kidney beans contain a toxin that can cause stomach upset. The longer you simmer the rajma in the masala, the more flavorful it gets — restaurants often simmer it for over an hour. Rajma tastes even better the next day after the flavors have had time to develop.

name: Butter Chicken
steps: Cut the chicken into medium-sized pieces. In a bowl, combine the yogurt, ginger garlic paste, red chili powder, turmeric, garam masala, and salt. Add the chicken pieces and mix until every piece is well coated. Cover and marinate in the refrigerator for at least 30 minutes, or up to overnight for best results.

When ready to cook, heat oil in a heavy pan or grill pan over high heat. Cook the marinated chicken pieces in batches, letting each side get nice charred marks — about 3-4 minutes per side. The chicken doesn't need to be cooked through at this point; it will finish cooking in the sauce. Set aside.
For the sauce, bring a pot of water to boil. Add the whole tomatoes and cashew nuts. Boil for 8-10 minutes until the tomatoes are soft and the skin starts peeling off.

Drain and let them cool slightly, then blend the tomatoes and cashews into a very smooth, silky puree. For the smoothest restaurant-style texture, strain this puree through a fine mesh sieve, pressing with the back of a spoon. Discard any solids.

In a large pan, melt the butter over medium heat. Once it foams, pour in the strained tomato-cashew puree. Add red chili powder, salt, and a teaspoon of sugar (this balances the acidity of the tomatoes).
Let the sauce simmer on medium-low heat for about 10 minutes, stirring occasionally, until it darkens slightly in color and the butter starts to separate around the edges.

Add the charred chicken pieces to the sauce. Stir gently to coat each piece, then simmer together on low heat for another 8-10 minutes so the chicken finishes cooking and absorbs the flavors of the sauce.
Finish by stirring in the fresh cream — this gives the dish its signature rich, velvety texture and beautiful orange color. Sprinkle garam masala on top.
Garnish with a swirl of cream and a few coriander leaves. Serve with hot naan, butter roti, or steamed basmati rice.
tips: The sugar is not optional — it's what transforms the sauce from tangy to that perfectly balanced, mildly sweet restaurant flavor. Straining the sauce is what separates homemade butter chicken from restaurant quality — it removes all the fibrous bits and gives you that signature smooth, silky gravy. For smoky flavor, heat a small piece of charcoal, place it in a steel bowl inside the pot, pour a teaspoon of ghee on it, and immediately cover for 2 minutes.

name: Palak Paneer
steps: Bring a large pot of water to a rolling boil. Add the washed spinach leaves and blanch for exactly 2 minutes — no longer, or the spinach will lose its bright green color and turn an unappealing dark green.
Immediately drain the spinach and plunge it into a bowl of ice-cold water (or run cold water over it). This ice bath is crucial — it stops the cooking instantly and locks in that vibrant green color.
Once cooled, squeeze out all the excess water from the spinach with your hands and blend it into a smooth, bright green puree. Set aside.

Cut the paneer into roughly 1-inch cubes. Optionally, you can lightly fry them in a little oil until golden on the outside — this gives them a nice firm texture that holds up in the gravy. Or soak them in warm salted water for 10 minutes to soften them.
Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Add cumin seeds and let them splutter. Add the minced garlic and slit green chilies. Sauté for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add the finely chopped onion and cook for 3-4 minutes until softened. Add the chopped tomato and cook for another 3-4 minutes until it breaks down.
Pour in the spinach puree. Add salt and garam masala. Stir well and let the gravy simmer on low heat for 5-6 minutes so all the flavors come together.
Gently add the paneer cubes to the spinach gravy. Stir carefully so the paneer doesn't break. Let everything simmer together for 3-4 minutes.
Finish with a generous swirl of cream, which adds richness and mellows the earthy spinach flavor. Serve hot with naan, roti, or rice.
tips: The blanch-and-ice-bath technique is the single most important step — it's what gives restaurant palak paneer that stunning bright green color vs the dull, brownish green of overcooked spinach. If you don't have ice, cold running water works too. Adding a pinch of sugar to the spinach while blanching can also help maintain the color.

name: Chicken Tacos
steps: Pat the chicken breasts dry with paper towels. In a bowl, combine cumin powder, red chili powder, a pinch of salt, and a squeeze of lime juice. Rub this mixture all over the chicken, coating both sides evenly.

Heat oil in a grill pan or heavy skillet over medium-high heat until the oil just starts to shimmer. Place the seasoned chicken breasts in the pan and cook without moving for about 5-6 minutes until the bottom is deeply golden and has nice grill marks.

Flip the chicken and cook the other side for another 5-6 minutes until cooked through (internal temperature should reach 75°C). Remove from heat and let the chicken rest on a cutting board for 5 minutes — this redistributes the juices so they don't all run out when you cut.
While the chicken rests, prepare the fresh salsa: dice the tomato and onion finely. Combine in a bowl with a squeeze of lime juice, chopped coriander leaves, and a pinch of salt. Toss gently.
Make quick guacamole: halve the avocado, scoop the flesh into a bowl, add a squeeze of lime, salt, and mash to your desired consistency with a fork.
Slice the rested chicken against the grain into thin strips. Cutting against the grain ensures each piece is tender and easy to bite through.
Warm the tortillas directly on a dry hot pan for about 20-30 seconds per side until they're pliable and have light brown spots. Stack and wrap in a clean kitchen towel to keep warm.
Assemble the tacos: lay out the warm tortillas, divide the sliced chicken among them, top with the fresh salsa, a dollop of guacamole, and a final squeeze of lime. Serve immediately.
tips: Letting the chicken rest after cooking is one of the most important steps that people skip — it makes a huge difference in juiciness. Warming tortillas on a dry pan brings them to life; cold tortillas from the package crack and don't fold well. For extra flavor, char the lime halves face-down on the hot pan before squeezing.

name: Grilled Cheese Sandwich
steps: Place the two bread slices on a cutting board. Spread a thin, even layer of butter on one side of each slice — edge to edge for an even golden crust.
Heat a non-stick pan or cast-iron skillet on medium-low heat. Let it warm for about 1 minute — medium-low prevents the bread from burning before the cheese melts.
Place one bread slice butter-side down on the pan. Immediately layer the cheese slices on top, covering most of the bread surface.
Top with the second bread slice butter-side up, making a sandwich. Press down very gently with a spatula to help the bread make full contact with the pan.
Cook undisturbed for 3–4 minutes until the bottom is deep golden brown. Lift a corner with the spatula to check color.
Flip carefully in one smooth motion. If the cheese is slipping, hold the top bread with your hand (careful of heat) while flipping.
Cook the second side for another 3–4 minutes until golden and the cheese is fully melted. If needed, cover the pan for 30 seconds to help the cheese melt faster.
Remove to a cutting board, rest for 1 minute (so the cheese sets slightly), then slice and serve hot.
tips: Medium-low heat is the secret: it gives you crisp bread and fully melted cheese without burning.

name: Chicken Caesar Salad
steps: Pat the chicken breast dry with paper towels. Season both sides with salt and black pepper.
Heat a pan over medium heat and add olive oil. Once the oil shimmers, place the chicken in the pan.
Cook undisturbed for 5–6 minutes until the bottom is golden brown, then flip and cook another 5–6 minutes until cooked through (center no longer pink).
Transfer chicken to a plate and let it rest for 5 minutes — this keeps it juicy when sliced.
Meanwhile, wash and dry the romaine lettuce thoroughly (wet lettuce makes dressing slide off). Chop into bite-sized pieces and place in a large bowl.
Add croutons and half the parmesan to the bowl.
Slice the rested chicken against the grain into thin strips and add to the salad.
Drizzle Caesar dressing over the salad. Toss gently with tongs until everything is evenly coated.
Finish with remaining parmesan on top and serve immediately.
tips: Dry lettuce is crucial for a restaurant-style Caesar — use a salad spinner if you have one.

name: Blueberry Muffins
steps: Preheat the oven to 180°C (350°F). Line a muffin tin with paper liners or lightly grease each cup.
In a large bowl, whisk together flour, sugar, baking powder, and salt until evenly combined.
In a separate bowl, whisk milk, egg, and melted butter (cooled slightly) until smooth.
Pour the wet ingredients into the dry ingredients. Stir gently with a spatula just until you no longer see dry flour — small lumps are normal and help keep muffins tender.
Add blueberries and fold them in gently, using as few strokes as possible to avoid crushing them and turning the batter purple.
Spoon the batter evenly into the muffin cups, filling each about 3/4 full so they rise nicely without overflowing.
Bake for 18–22 minutes until the tops are lightly golden and a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean (a few moist crumbs are okay).
Let muffins cool in the tray for 5 minutes, then move to a rack to cool completely.
tips: Overmixing is the #1 cause of tough muffins — stop mixing as soon as the flour disappears.

name: Greek Yogurt Parfait
steps: Wash the strawberries and pat them dry. Slice them into thin pieces so they layer neatly and are easy to eat with a spoon.
In a small bowl, stir the honey into the Greek yogurt until smooth and lightly sweetened.
Choose a tall glass or bowl. Spoon a layer of yogurt into the bottom and spread it out so the layer is even.
Add a layer of granola on top of the yogurt. Use an even layer so every bite has crunch.
Add a layer of sliced strawberries, distributing them around the edges for a nice look.
Repeat the layers (yogurt, granola, strawberries) until you reach the top.
Finish with a small handful of granola on top for maximum crunch.
Serve immediately so the granola stays crisp.
tips: If you’re meal-prepping, keep granola separate and add right before eating to prevent sogginess.

name: Banana Bread
steps: Preheat the oven to 175°C (350°F). Grease a loaf pan and line it with parchment paper for easy removal.
Peel the bananas and mash them in a bowl with a fork until mostly smooth. A few small lumps are fine and add texture.
In a separate bowl, whisk together flour, baking soda, and salt so the leavening is evenly distributed.
Melt the butter and let it cool slightly so it doesn’t scramble the egg.
In a large bowl, mix melted butter and sugar until glossy. Add the egg and whisk until the mixture looks smooth and slightly thick.
Add the mashed bananas and stir until combined.
Add the dry ingredients to the wet ingredients and fold gently just until no dry flour remains. Do not overmix — that makes banana bread tough.
Pour batter into the prepared loaf pan and smooth the top.
Bake for 45–55 minutes until a toothpick inserted in the center comes out clean or with a few moist crumbs.
Cool in the pan for 10 minutes, then lift out and cool completely before slicing for clean slices.
tips: Use very ripe bananas with brown spots — they’re sweeter and give better banana flavor.

name: Chocolate Chip Cookies
steps: Preheat the oven to 180°C (350°F). Line a baking sheet with parchment paper.
In a bowl, whisk together flour, baking soda, and salt. Set aside.
In a separate large bowl, cream the softened butter with both sugars using a whisk or spoon until the mixture looks lighter and fluffy.
Add the egg and mix until fully incorporated and smooth.
Add the dry ingredients to the wet ingredients and fold gently until you no longer see dry flour. Stop as soon as it comes together.
Fold in the chocolate chips evenly so every cookie has chips.
Scoop tablespoon-sized balls onto the baking sheet, leaving space between them for spreading.
Bake for 10–12 minutes until the edges are lightly golden but the centers still look slightly soft.
Let cookies cool on the baking sheet for 5 minutes (they continue setting), then transfer to a rack to cool completely.
tips: For softer cookies, pull them out when the center still looks slightly underbaked — they firm up as they cool.

name: Mac and Cheese
steps: Bring a large pot of salted water to a rolling boil. Add macaroni and cook until al dente according to the package directions.
Reserve 1/2 cup of pasta water, then drain the pasta and set aside.
In the same pot (or a saucepan), melt butter over medium heat.
Add flour to the melted butter and whisk continuously for 1 minute to cook out the raw flour taste. You should get a smooth paste (roux).
Slowly pour in the milk while whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Keep whisking until the mixture becomes smooth.
Cook for 3–5 minutes, stirring often, until the sauce thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon.
Lower the heat and add cheddar cheese in handfuls, stirring after each addition until fully melted and smooth.
Season with salt and black pepper. If the sauce is too thick, loosen it with a splash of reserved pasta water.
Add the cooked macaroni and stir until every piece is coated in cheese sauce.
Serve immediately while hot and creamy.
tips: Add cheese off the heat or on low heat — high heat can make the sauce grainy.

name: Chicken Noodle Soup
steps: Dice the onion, slice the carrots and celery into small pieces so they cook evenly. Mince the garlic.
Heat olive oil in a large pot over medium heat. Add onion, carrot, and celery and sauté for 5 minutes until softened and aromatic.
Add minced garlic and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant — don’t let it brown.
Pour in chicken stock and bring to a gentle boil.
Add chicken pieces. Reduce to a simmer and cook for 12–15 minutes until the chicken is cooked through.
Remove the chicken to a plate and shred it using two forks. This keeps the soup clear and prevents overcooked chicken.
Return shredded chicken to the pot. Add noodles and simmer until noodles are tender (usually 6–10 minutes depending on the noodle).
Taste and adjust salt and pepper. If it tastes flat, add a pinch more salt first — it often fixes the flavor.
Serve hot. If storing for later, consider cooking noodles separately so they don’t get mushy.
tips: For meal-prep, keep noodles separate and add when reheating to maintain perfect texture.

name: Veggie Stir Fry
steps: Cut broccoli into small florets, slice the carrot thinly, and slice capsicum into strips so everything cooks quickly and evenly.
In a small bowl, mix soy sauce, water, and cornstarch until smooth — this is your quick stir-fry sauce that thickens and coats the vegetables.
Heat a wok or large pan on high heat until very hot. Add oil and swirl to coat.
Add minced garlic and stir constantly for 10–15 seconds until fragrant.
Add carrots first (they take the longest). Stir-fry for 1 minute.
Add broccoli and stir-fry for 2 minutes, tossing continuously so it doesn’t burn. If needed, splash 1–2 tbsp water and cover for 30 seconds to steam slightly.
Add capsicum and stir-fry for another 1–2 minutes until crisp-tender.
Pour the sauce around the edges of the hot pan (it heats instantly). Toss quickly until the sauce thickens and glazes the vegetables.
Finish with sesame oil for aroma, toss once, and serve immediately with rice or noodles.
tips: High heat + fast cooking is key — you want crisp-tender veggies, not soggy ones.

name: Chicken Fried Rice
steps: Use cold, day-old rice if possible. If using fresh rice, spread it on a plate for 10 minutes to dry slightly so it doesn’t clump.
Dice the carrot small and keep peas ready. Cut chicken into small bite-sized pieces for fast cooking.
Heat a large pan or wok on high heat. Add 1 tbsp oil and swirl to coat.
Add chicken and stir-fry for 3–4 minutes until cooked through and lightly browned. Remove to a plate.
Add remaining oil. Crack eggs into the pan and scramble quickly, breaking into small pieces.
Add carrots and peas and stir-fry for 1–2 minutes until the carrots start to soften.
Add the rice and break up clumps with your spatula. Toss continuously so it heats evenly.
Return chicken to the pan. Add soy sauce around the edges and toss to distribute evenly.
Season with salt and black pepper. Toss for another 1 minute until everything is hot and well mixed.
Serve immediately while hot.
tips: The secret is dry rice — wet rice turns fried rice into mush.

name: Shakshuka
steps: Finely chop onion and capsicum. Mince the garlic. Roughly chop tomatoes (they will break down into sauce).
Heat olive oil in a wide pan over medium heat. Add onion and capsicum and sauté for 4–5 minutes until softened and slightly sweet.
Add garlic and stir for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add chopped tomatoes, paprika, cumin powder, and salt. Stir well and let the mixture simmer for 6–8 minutes until it thickens into a chunky sauce.
Use the back of your spoon to crush some tomatoes against the pan to speed up sauce formation.
Make 4 small wells in the sauce with your spoon. Crack an egg into each well carefully so the yolks stay intact.
Cover the pan with a lid and cook for 4–6 minutes until whites are set but yolks are still slightly runny (or longer if you prefer firmer yolks).
Turn off heat and rest for 1 minute, then serve straight from the pan with bread.
tips: Keep heat moderate so the sauce reduces without burning; covering is what cooks the egg tops evenly.

name: Chilaquiles
steps: Heat a wide pan over medium heat and pour in salsa. Let it simmer for 2–3 minutes so it thickens slightly and tastes more cooked.
Add tortilla chips to the simmering salsa and toss gently to coat. Aim for chips that soften slightly but still hold some crunch.
Cook for 2 minutes, stirring carefully so chips don’t completely break into crumbs.
In a separate pan, heat oil over medium heat and fry eggs to your liking (sunny-side up works best).
Turn off heat under the chips and immediately sprinkle shredded cheddar over the top so it melts from the residual heat.
Plate the chilaquiles and top each serving with a fried egg.
Finish with small dollops of sour cream across the top.
Serve immediately while the chips still have texture.
tips: Don’t overcook the chips in salsa — 2–3 minutes is enough for the perfect soft-crunch balance.

name: Oatmeal with Peanut Butter & Banana
steps: Add oats, milk, and a pinch of salt to a small pot. Stir once so oats are fully submerged.
Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring occasionally to prevent sticking at the bottom.
Cook for 3–5 minutes until the oats soften and the mixture thickens to your preferred consistency.
Turn off heat and let it sit for 1 minute — oatmeal thickens slightly as it stands.
Slice banana into rounds.
Spoon oatmeal into a bowl. Stir in peanut butter while the oatmeal is hot so it melts and becomes creamy.
Top with banana slices and drizzle honey over the top.
Serve immediately.
tips: A tiny pinch of salt makes oatmeal taste sweeter and more balanced even with honey.

name: Breakfast Burrito
steps: Dice the potato into very small cubes (about 1/4 inch). Small cubes cook quickly and evenly.
Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Add potatoes and a pinch of salt and cook for 6–7 minutes, stirring occasionally, until golden and tender.
Add finely chopped onion and cook for 1–2 minutes until softened and slightly sweet.
In a bowl, whisk eggs with salt and black pepper until fully blended.
Push the potato-onion mix to one side of the pan. Pour eggs into the empty side and scramble gently.
When eggs are mostly set but still soft, mix them with the potatoes.
Warm the tortilla in a dry pan for 20 seconds per side until pliable.
Place the filling in the center of the tortilla. Sprinkle cheese over the hot filling so it melts.
Fold the sides in, then roll tightly into a burrito. Optionally toast the seam-side down in the pan for 30–60 seconds to seal.
Serve hot.
tips: Cut potatoes small — big cubes make the burrito take forever and the eggs overcook while waiting.

name: Belgian Waffles
steps: Preheat the waffle iron so it’s fully hot before batter hits the surface — this is what creates crisp edges.
In a bowl, whisk flour, baking powder, sugar, and salt until evenly combined.
In another bowl, whisk milk, melted butter (cooled slightly), and eggs until smooth.
Pour wet ingredients into dry ingredients and stir gently just until combined. A few small lumps are okay.
Lightly grease the waffle iron if needed (some non-stick irons don’t require it).
Pour batter into the center of the iron (amount depends on your machine) and close the lid without pressing down hard.
Cook until the waffle is deep golden and steam output decreases — usually 3–5 minutes.
Remove carefully and place on a rack (not a plate) so it stays crisp.
Repeat with remaining batter and serve warm.
tips: Putting waffles on a rack keeps them crisp — plates trap steam and make them soggy.

name: Caprese Sandwich
steps: Slice the tomato into thin rounds. Lightly salt the slices and let them sit for 2 minutes — this concentrates flavor and prevents a watery sandwich.
Slice mozzarella into even slices so it layers neatly.
Place bread slices on a board. Drizzle a small amount of olive oil on the inner sides of the bread.
Layer mozzarella on one slice, then tomato slices, then basil leaves. Distribute basil so every bite has aroma.
Drizzle balsamic glaze lightly over the top layer.
Close the sandwich, press gently, and slice in half.
Serve immediately. Optionally toast lightly if you want warmth, but traditional Caprese is served fresh.
tips: Salt the tomatoes briefly — it upgrades the flavor and keeps the sandwich from getting soggy.

name: Hummus & Pita
steps: Drain and rinse chickpeas thoroughly to remove the canning liquid taste.
Add chickpeas, tahini, lemon juice, garlic, and salt to a blender or food processor.
Blend for 30 seconds, then scrape down the sides so everything blends evenly.
With the blender running, drizzle in olive oil slowly. This emulsifies the hummus and makes it creamy.
If the hummus is too thick, add 1–2 tbsp water and blend again until smooth.
Taste and adjust: more lemon for brightness, more salt for flavor, more olive oil for richness.
Warm pita briefly (optional), cut into wedges, and serve with hummus.
tips: Adding a little water while blending makes hummus extra smooth and fluffy.

name: Garlic Bread
steps: Preheat the oven to 200°C (400°F).
Mince the garlic finely. Chop parsley.
In a bowl, mix softened butter, minced garlic, parsley, and a pinch of salt until evenly combined.
Slice the bread in half lengthwise (or into thick slices if using a baguette).
Spread the garlic butter mixture evenly over the cut sides — cover all the way to the edges.
Place bread on a baking tray and bake for 6–8 minutes until the edges turn golden and crisp.
For extra browning, broil for 30–60 seconds at the end — watch closely to avoid burning.
Slice and serve hot.
tips: Broil at the end for 30 seconds for that restaurant-style crisp, but don’t take your eyes off it.

name: French Fries (Oven)
steps: Preheat oven to 220°C (425°F). Line a baking tray with parchment paper.
Cut potatoes into even sticks so they bake at the same rate. Aim for about 1/4-inch thickness.
Rinse the cut potatoes under cold water to remove surface starch. This helps crispiness.
Pat potatoes completely dry with a towel — moisture is the enemy of crisp fries.
Toss potatoes with oil, salt, and paprika until every piece is lightly coated.
Spread fries in a single layer on the tray with space between them. Crowding causes steaming, not crisping.
Bake for 15 minutes, flip fries, then bake another 12–15 minutes until golden and crisp.
Taste and adjust salt while hot. Serve immediately.
tips: Drying the potatoes thoroughly is what makes oven fries actually crisp instead of soft.

name: New York Cheesecake (No-Bake)
steps: Crush digestive biscuits into fine crumbs (use a zip bag + rolling pin or a processor).
Melt butter and mix it into the crumbs until the mixture looks like wet sand and holds shape when pressed.
Press the crumb mixture firmly into the bottom of a bowl or small pan to form an even base. Chill in the fridge while you make the filling.
In a bowl, beat cream cheese with sugar until completely smooth — no lumps. This is important for a silky cheesecake texture.
Add vanilla extract and mix again.
In a separate bowl, whip heavy cream until it forms stiff peaks (it should hold its shape).
Fold whipped cream into the cream cheese mixture gently in 2–3 batches. Use a spatula and fold, don’t stir, so you keep the airiness.
Spoon the filling over the chilled base and smooth the top.
Chill for at least 4 hours (overnight is best) so it sets fully before slicing.
tips: Let cream cheese come to room temperature — cold cream cheese is the main reason for lumpy cheesecake filling.

name: Apple Crumble
steps: Preheat oven to 190°C (375°F).
Peel apples (optional), then slice into bite-sized pieces and place in a baking dish.
Add sugar and cinnamon to the apples and toss well so every piece is coated.
In a bowl, combine flour and oats. Add cold butter cut into small cubes.
Use your fingers to rub butter into the flour-oat mixture until it becomes crumbly — you want pea-sized crumbles.
Sprinkle the crumble topping evenly over the apples, covering the surface.
Bake for 25–35 minutes until the top is golden and the apple filling is bubbling at the edges.
Let it rest for 5 minutes before serving so the juices thicken slightly.
tips: Cold butter makes the best crumble topping — it creates crisp, golden crumbs instead of a paste.

name: Tiramisu (Quick)
steps: Brew strong coffee and let it cool to room temperature. Hot coffee will make the ladyfingers too soggy too fast.
In a bowl, whisk mascarpone with sugar until smooth and creamy.
In another bowl, whip heavy cream until it forms soft peaks (it should be fluffy but not grainy).
Fold whipped cream into mascarpone gently in 2–3 additions to keep the mixture airy.
Dip each ladyfinger quickly into the cooled coffee — 1–2 seconds per side. They should be moist but not collapsing.
Arrange a single layer of dipped ladyfingers in the bottom of a dish.
Spread half the cream mixture evenly over the ladyfingers.
Repeat with another layer of coffee-dipped ladyfingers, then spread remaining cream on top.
Dust the top generously with cocoa powder using a sieve for an even layer.
Chill for at least 4 hours so the flavors meld and the dessert slices cleanly.
tips: Dip fast — ladyfingers soak up liquid instantly, and over-soaking makes tiramisu watery.

name: Gulab Jamun (Instant Mix Style)
steps: First make the sugar syrup: in a pot, combine sugar and water. Bring to a boil, then simmer for 5 minutes until slightly sticky (not one-string thick). Add cardamom and turn off heat. Keep warm.
In a bowl, mix milk powder, flour, and baking powder evenly.
Add milk slowly and knead gently to form a soft dough. The dough should be smooth and slightly sticky, not dry. Rest for 5 minutes.
Grease your palms with a little oil. Divide dough into small portions and roll into smooth balls with no cracks — cracks cause jamuns to break while frying.
Heat oil on low-medium heat. The oil must be medium hot, not smoking. Test by dropping a tiny bit of dough — it should rise slowly.
Fry jamuns in batches, stirring gently, until they turn deep golden brown evenly (about 6–8 minutes). Keep the heat moderate so the inside cooks fully.
Remove and drain briefly, then immediately add hot jamuns to warm syrup.
Soak for at least 1 hour so they absorb syrup and become soft and juicy.
Serve warm or at room temperature.
tips: Low-medium frying is crucial — high heat browns the outside while the inside stays raw.

name: Churros
steps: In a pot, combine water, butter, sugar, and salt. Bring to a boil so the butter melts completely.
Reduce heat to low and add flour all at once. Stir vigorously with a wooden spoon until a smooth dough forms and pulls away from the sides of the pot.
Cook the dough for 1 minute, stirring, to dry it slightly — this helps churros crisp up.
Transfer dough to a bowl and let it cool for 5 minutes so it’s safe to handle.
Heat oil in a deep pan to medium heat. The oil should be hot enough that dough sizzles, but not so hot that it browns instantly.
Spoon dough into a piping bag with a star tip. Pipe strips into the oil, cutting with scissors to desired length.
Fry in batches, turning, until deep golden brown and crisp (about 3–4 minutes).
Drain on paper towels. While hot, roll churros in a mix of sugar and cinnamon.
Serve immediately.
tips: Cook the flour dough for 1 minute — drying it slightly is what gives churros a crisp shell.

name: Miso Soup
steps: Bring water to a gentle simmer in a pot — do not boil aggressively.
Add seaweed and simmer for 1 minute to rehydrate and flavor the broth.
Cut tofu into small cubes and slice spring onion thinly.
Add tofu to the pot and warm for 2 minutes on low heat.
In a small bowl, mix miso paste with a few spoonfuls of hot broth until completely smooth. This prevents miso clumps.
Turn the heat to the lowest setting (or off). Stir the dissolved miso into the soup.
Do not boil after adding miso — boiling destroys aroma and can make it taste harsh.
Serve immediately topped with spring onion.
tips: Never boil miso after adding it — keep the heat low to preserve flavor and aroma.

name: Teriyaki Chicken Bowl
steps: Cut chicken into bite-sized pieces so it cooks quickly and evenly.
In a bowl, mix soy sauce, honey, minced garlic, grated ginger, water, and cornstarch until smooth. This is your teriyaki sauce.
Heat oil in a pan over medium-high heat. Add chicken pieces in a single layer.
Cook for 3–4 minutes, then flip and cook another 3–4 minutes until browned and cooked through.
Reduce heat to medium. Pour the sauce into the pan and stir continuously.
Let it simmer for 2–3 minutes until the sauce thickens and becomes glossy, coating every piece of chicken.
Steam or microwave broccoli until bright green and tender-crisp (about 2–3 minutes).
Assemble bowls: rice on the bottom, broccoli on the side, teriyaki chicken on top.
Spoon extra sauce over the rice and serve.
tips: Cornstarch must be fully dissolved before heating — otherwise you’ll get lumps in the sauce.

name: Pad Thai
steps: Soak rice noodles in warm water for 8–10 minutes until pliable but not fully soft. Drain well — they will finish cooking in the pan.
Mix tamarind paste, soy sauce, and brown sugar in a bowl until the sugar dissolves. This is the Pad Thai sauce.
Heat a wok or large pan on high heat. Add oil and swirl.
Add minced garlic and stir for 10 seconds until fragrant.
Add shrimp and stir-fry for 2–3 minutes until they turn pink and are just cooked through. Push shrimp to the side.
Crack eggs into the pan and scramble quickly, then mix with the shrimp.
Add drained noodles and pour sauce over them. Toss continuously for 2–3 minutes until noodles absorb sauce and become tender.
Add bean sprouts and toss for 30 seconds — you want them slightly warmed but still crunchy.
Plate and top with crushed peanuts. Serve with lime wedges to squeeze over the top.
tips: Do not over-soak noodles — slightly underdone noodles finish perfectly in the sauce without turning mushy.

name: Bibimbap (Simple)
steps: Julienne the carrot, slice cucumber thinly, and keep spinach ready. Prep everything first — bibimbap comes together quickly.
Heat a pan with a little oil. Sauté carrots for 2 minutes until slightly softened but still bright. Remove to a plate.
In the same pan, sauté spinach for 1–2 minutes just until wilted. Add a tiny splash of sesame oil and remove.
Add ground beef to the pan and cook over medium-high heat until browned, breaking it up into small bits.
Add soy sauce and 1 tsp sesame oil to the beef and stir for 30 seconds to coat and season. Turn off heat.
Fry eggs sunny-side up (or to preference) in a separate pan so the yolk stays runny.
Mix gochujang with 1 tsp sesame oil and a tiny splash of water to make it easier to drizzle.
Assemble bowls: rice at the bottom, then arrange beef, carrots, spinach, and cucumber in separate sections on top.
Top each bowl with a fried egg and spoon gochujang sauce over the top.
Mix everything thoroughly before eating — that’s the bibimbap experience.
tips: Arrange toppings separately, then mix at the table — it keeps textures distinct until the moment you eat.

name: Chicken Shawarma Wrap
steps: In a bowl, mix yogurt, minced garlic, cumin powder, paprika, and salt to make the marinade.
Add chicken and mix thoroughly so every piece is coated. Marinate for at least 10 minutes (longer is better).
Heat a pan over medium-high heat. Add the marinated chicken in a single layer.
Cook for 4–5 minutes, then flip and cook another 4–5 minutes until browned and cooked through.
Remove chicken to a board and rest 3 minutes, then slice thinly for a shawarma-style texture.
Slice cucumber and tomato into thin pieces.
Make quick tahini sauce: mix tahini with lemon juice and 1–2 tbsp water until it becomes creamy and drizzleable. Add a pinch of salt if needed.
Warm pita briefly so it becomes soft and flexible.
Assemble: place chicken and vegetables on the pita, drizzle tahini sauce, then roll tightly into a wrap.
Serve immediately.
tips: Let chicken rest before slicing — it keeps it juicier and makes thinner slices easier.

name: Falafel Bowl
steps: Drain and rinse chickpeas well, then pat them dry. Less moisture helps the falafel hold shape.
Add chickpeas, onion, garlic, cumin, coriander leaves, and flour to a food processor. Pulse until you get a coarse paste that holds together when pressed — do not puree completely smooth.
Let the mixture sit for 5 minutes so flour hydrates and binds the mixture better.
Form small balls or patties using damp hands to prevent sticking.
Heat oil in a pan over medium heat. Fry falafel patties for 3–4 minutes per side until deep golden and crisp.
Drain on paper towels.
Make tahini sauce by mixing tahini and lemon juice with a splash of water until creamy and pourable.
Assemble bowls with rice at the bottom, top with falafel, and drizzle tahini sauce over the top.
Serve warm.
tips: Don’t over-blend the falafel mix — a slightly coarse texture helps it stay crisp and hold together.

name: Tomato Basil Soup
steps: Chop onion and mince garlic. Roughly chop tomatoes.
Heat olive oil in a pot over medium heat. Add onion and sauté for 4–5 minutes until soft and translucent.
Add garlic and cook for 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add tomatoes and salt. Stir well and cook for 10–12 minutes until tomatoes break down and become saucy.
Turn off heat and blend the soup until smooth (use an immersion blender or a blender in batches).
Return to low heat and stir in basil leaves so they infuse flavor. Simmer 2 minutes.
Stir in cream for richness and a velvety finish.
Taste and adjust salt. Serve hot with bread.
tips: Blending while the tomatoes are fully softened gives you the smoothest soup without needing a strainer.

name: Beef Chili
steps: Finely chop onion and mince garlic. Chop tomatoes.
Heat oil in a pot over medium-high heat. Add onion and sauté 3–4 minutes until softened.
Add garlic and cook 30 seconds until fragrant.
Add ground beef and cook, breaking it up with a spoon, until browned and no pink remains.
Add chili powder and cumin powder and stir for 30 seconds to toast the spices and deepen flavor.
Add tomatoes and salt. Cook 8–10 minutes until tomatoes break down and form a thick base.
Add kidney beans and 1 cup water (or stock). Bring to a boil, then reduce to a low simmer.
Simmer uncovered 15–20 minutes, stirring occasionally, until chili thickens and flavors meld.
Taste and adjust salt/spice. Serve hot.
tips: Toast the spices in the beef fat for 30 seconds — it makes chili taste dramatically richer.

name: Baked Salmon with Lemon
steps: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Line a baking tray with foil or parchment.
Pat salmon fillets dry. Dry fish browns better and doesn’t steam.
Place salmon on the tray skin-side down. Season with salt and black pepper.
Mince garlic and mix with olive oil. Spoon the garlic-oil over the salmon evenly.
Slice lemon into rounds. Place lemon slices on top of the salmon to infuse flavor while baking.
Bake for 10–13 minutes depending on thickness. Salmon is done when it flakes easily and the center is slightly translucent but not raw.
Rest for 2 minutes, then squeeze fresh lemon juice over the top for a bright finish.
Serve immediately.
tips: Don’t overbake — salmon goes from perfect to dry fast. Pull it when it flakes and finishes cooking while resting.

name: Veggie Quesadilla
steps: Slice capsicum and onion thinly so they cook quickly inside the quesadilla.
Heat a pan over medium heat with a tiny bit of oil. Add onion and capsicum and sauté for 3–4 minutes until softened. Season with a pinch of salt.
Place a tortilla flat on the pan. Sprinkle half the cheese evenly over it.
Add the cooked veggies on top of the cheese, spreading them so each slice has filling.
Sprinkle remaining cheese over the veggies — cheese on both sides helps glue the quesadilla together.
Top with the second tortilla and press gently.
Cook for 2–3 minutes until the bottom is golden and the cheese starts melting, then flip carefully and cook another 2–3 minutes.
Remove, rest for 1 minute, slice into wedges, and serve hot.
tips: Cheese on both sides is the trick — it melts and binds the tortilla so the filling doesn’t fall out.

name: Roasted Chickpeas
steps: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Line a baking tray with parchment paper.
Drain and rinse chickpeas, then pat completely dry with a towel. Dry chickpeas get crispy; wet ones stay soft.
Spread chickpeas on the tray and roast for 10 minutes to dry them out further.
Remove tray, drizzle olive oil, and toss to coat evenly.
Sprinkle paprika and salt and toss again so seasoning coats every chickpea.
Return to oven and roast 12–15 minutes, shaking the tray halfway, until crispy and lightly browned.
Let cool for 5 minutes — they crisp up more as they cool.
Snack immediately or store uncovered for best crispness.
tips: Dry them thoroughly — it’s the difference between crunchy roasted chickpeas and chewy ones.

name: Classic Nachos
steps: Preheat oven to 200°C (400°F). Line a tray with parchment paper.
Spread chips in an even layer with minimal overlap so cheese melts evenly.
Sprinkle cheese generously across the chips, aiming for coverage across the whole tray.
Slice jalapeño thinly and scatter on top.
Bake for 6–8 minutes until cheese is fully melted and bubbling.
Remove from oven and immediately spoon salsa across the top in small pockets.
Add small dollops of sour cream over the nachos.
Serve immediately while hot.
tips: Keep toppings like salsa/sour cream for after baking — they stay fresh and don’t make chips soggy.

name: Berry Ice Cream (No-Churn)
steps: If berries are large (like strawberries), chop them into small pieces so they distribute evenly.
Whip heavy cream in a large bowl until stiff peaks form — it should hold shape and look fluffy.
In a separate bowl, mix condensed milk with vanilla extract until smooth.
Fold the condensed milk mixture into the whipped cream gently in 2–3 batches. Use a spatula and fold to keep the mixture airy.
Fold in the berries gently so they don’t get crushed completely.
Transfer mixture to a container and smooth the top.
Cover and freeze for at least 6 hours until firm.
Scoop and serve.
tips: Stiff peaks in the cream are what create the ice-cream texture without a machine — don’t stop at soft peaks.

name: Mango Lassi
steps: If using fresh mango, peel and cut into chunks. If using frozen mango, measure it out and let it sit for 2 minutes to soften slightly for easier blending.
Add mango, yogurt, milk, sugar, and a pinch of cardamom to a blender.
Blend on high for 30–45 seconds until completely smooth and frothy.
Taste and adjust sweetness by adding more sugar if needed. Adjust thickness by adding a splash of milk if too thick.
Pour into glasses and serve immediately chilled.
tips: Using chilled yogurt and milk gives you a colder, thicker lassi without needing ice.

name: Rice Pudding
steps: Rinse rice under cold water until the water runs mostly clear. This removes extra starch and prevents gumminess.
Add rice and milk to a pot along with a pinch of salt. Bring to a gentle simmer over medium heat, stirring occasionally so milk doesn’t scorch.
Once simmering, reduce heat to low. Cook for 20–25 minutes, stirring every 2–3 minutes, until rice is tender and the mixture thickens.
Add sugar and cinnamon and stir well. Cook another 3–5 minutes until the sugar dissolves and the pudding becomes creamy.
Turn off heat and stir in vanilla extract.
Let it rest for 5 minutes — it thickens as it cools.
Serve warm or chilled.
tips: Stir frequently on low heat — milk can scorch quickly and ruin the flavor.

name: Overnight Oats
steps: Choose a jar or container with a lid. Add rolled oats and chia seeds to the jar.
Pour in milk and add honey. Stir thoroughly so chia seeds don’t clump — clumps make the texture uneven.
Add berries on top (or mix them in if you prefer flavored oats throughout).
Seal the jar and refrigerate for at least 6 hours or overnight so the oats hydrate and soften.
In the morning, stir the oats again. If it’s too thick, add a splash more milk and mix.
Taste and adjust sweetness with a little more honey if needed.
Eat cold, or microwave for 30–45 seconds if you prefer it warm.
tips: Stir once before refrigerating and once before eating — it guarantees a smooth, pudding-like texture.
